channel.title: PYRANA Daily
channel.description: Daily AI agent research, narrated. Two hosts unpack the day's most interesting papers from PYRANA Research Intelligence.
channel.itunes:author: Zeroth Agents

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — May 14, 2026
item.description:
Four papers land on the same axis: how to make agent reasoning auditable and reproducible when the underlying model is stochastic. The episode covers a multi-hop retrieval system that externalises reasoning as executable Python, a multi-agent patent framework that uses cross-methodology convergence to rank claims, a memory study showing that model-driven memory rewriting degrades accuracy over time, and a scientific-workflow paper making the empirical case for deterministic tool dispatch over generative code.

Three of the four papers map directly onto active Cortex work. The PyRAG executable-reasoning pattern is a working prototype for the Text2Cypher pathway: emit a Python program, log its execution trace as a CxU provenance event, and every multi-hop query becomes a replayable artifact. The memory paper is a concrete warning against shipping CORTEXLEARNINGENABLED on by default — repeated LLM rewrites corrupt previously accurate knowledge. And the deterministic-mediation paper is the strongest empirical backing yet for the inproc-MCP tool design: typed tools beat generated code on reproducibility and error traceability.

Papers covered:

  • Retrieval is Cheap, Show Me the Code: Executable Multi-Hop Reasoning for Retrieval-Augmented Generation — Turns multi-hop RAG reasoning into executable Python programs, yielding +25.5 exact-match on Bamboogle with no fine-tuning.
  • IdeaForge: A Knowledge Graph-Grounded Multi-Agent Framework for Cross-Methodology Innovation Analysis and Patent Claim Generation — Multiple agents using distinct innovation frameworks write to a shared knowledge graph; convergent claims are ranked higher for patent drafting.
  • Useful Memories Become Faulty When Continuously Updated by LLMs — Continuous LLM-driven memory rewriting degrades accuracy of previously correct knowledge over successive updates.
  • It's not the Language Model, it's the Tool: Deterministic Mediation for Scientific Workflows — Replacing generative code with typed, deterministic tool selection improves reproducibility and error traceability in scientific agent workflows.

Takeaways for our platform:

  • Wire the PyRAG Plan Agent pattern into the Text2Cypher pathway so multi-hop queries emit a Python execution trace logged as a CxU provenance event — this delivers the replayable audit trail the governed-agent story requires. Add a complexity router so single-hop lookups bypass program-synthesis overhead entirely.
  • Gate CORTEXLEARNINGENABLED to off-by-default and require explicit versioned checkpoints before any model-driven CxU rewrite is committed; the memory paper shows that incremental rewrites silently corrupt accurate claims without triggering obvious failure signals.
  • Use IdeaForge's convergence-tagging pattern as a reference architecture for the regulated-tenant demo: when multiple CxU extraction agents independently surface the same claim from different source types, promote that claim's confidence score and surface the agreement count in the audit trail.

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — May 12, 2026
item.description: Cold open. If you're building agents this quarter and you only remember one number from today's batch, make it this: Shepherd, out of Stanford and Northeastern, gets you a 5× faster process-plus-filesystem fork than docker commit on a 5.8 gigabyte image, with over 95% prompt-cache reuse on replay. And that's not a microbenchmark flex — they ride it into a live supervision jump from 28.8 to 54.7% j

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — May 8, 2026
item.description: Okay, here's the thing that got me out of my chair this morning. A paper landed today claiming a three-strategy retrieval architecture where the top tier is Text2Cypher — natural language straight into a graph query — and PYRANA's own digest scored it 8.05 out of 10, the highest of the day. The framing is literally "superintelligent retrieval agent." Now, we don't have benchmark numbers — we only

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — April 30, 2026
item.description: Quick programming note before we get into it — today's episode is going to be different. We owe you an honest conversation about what happened with our prep. Yeah, so normally on a Thursday we'd come in with six papers, mechanisms walked through, numbers on the table. Today we have a problem. The source pull broke. Walk me through what you mean by broke, because I think the listener deserves the f

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — April 29, 2026
item.description: Welcome back to PYRANA Daily. It's April 29th, 2026, and today we've got four papers that, taken together, basically sketch out what a serious agent stack looks like in 2026 — orchestration, memory, governance, and a real regulated deployment. The one I want to lead with: a framework called OxyGent that tries to collapse agents, tools, LLM calls, and entire reasoning flows into one interchangeable

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — April 28, 2026
item.description: Okay, so if you only remember one thing from today's episode, remember this phrase: Attention Latch. There's a paper out today that names a failure mode I think a lot of you have been hitting in production without knowing what to call it. Your agent anchors on a constraint from turn three of a fifty-turn conversation, the user updates the requirement at turn twenty, and the agent just… keeps optim

item.title: PYRANA Research Intelligence — April 27, 2026
item.description: Okay, so if you've been paying what people are starting to call the memory tax — you know, the multi-second ingestion latency every time your agent writes something to its long-term store — there's a paper today that basically says: you don't have to. Yeah, this is the Memanto paper from Abtahi and colleagues, and the headline number is wild. 89.8% on LongMemEval, 87.1% on LoCoMo, sub-90 milliseco
